---
title: "urge surfing practice"
format: html
---

## Urge Surfing Practice

Urge surfing is a mindfulness-based practice for staying present during cravings without acting on them. Instead of fighting the urge or obeying it, you observe it as a changing experience.

This works because urges shift over time. When you stop reinforcing urges with drinking, they lose strength across repeated episodes.

Cravings are influenced by learning, context, and emotional state. That means they can be understood and managed, even when they feel intense. The strongest part of an urge is usually brief; what changes outcomes is how you respond during that window.

This activity builds two protective skills: early recognition (spotting triggers and escalation) and response flexibility (choosing actions that reduce risk). Practicing these skills repeatedly lowers fear of cravings and increases confidence in your ability to stay sober under pressure.

::: callout
### Instructions
Use this script during a craving. Stay with each step for 30-60 seconds.
:::

1. Name the urge: "I am noticing a strong urge to drink."
2. Notice where it shows up in your body.
3. Breathe slowly and describe sensations without judging.
4. Remind yourself: "This wave will pass."
5. Stay with the urge for 2-3 minutes, then choose a safe action.

### Practice Record

| Trigger | Peak intensity (0-10) | Minutes until it eased | Safe action I chose |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |
| | | | |
| | | | |
